Your response - 
Participant 8 Reasoning:
results.raw_response:
<reasoning>
First, recall that a bond is a zero-coupon bond when it pays no coupons. The price of a zero-coupon bond(I) can be determined using the formula:
Price = FV / (1 + r)^n
Here, FV stands for face value, r is the yield provided,
n is the time for which the bond is purchased for. These values are substituted into the formula.
FV = $100, r = 4% = 4 / 100 = 0.04, and n = 5 years.
Substituting these values into the formula: 
Price = 100 / (1 + 0.04)^5
Price = 100 / (1.04)^5
Price = 100 / 1.21552
Price ≈ $82.43
The answer is C : $82.
</reasoning>
<answer>C</answer>

To determine the relationship between the power input and power output of an ideal transformer, we need to understand how an ideal transformer works and what principles it implements.
A transformer works on the principle of electromagnetic induction. In a transformer, a current-carrying coil (primary coil) is connected to a power source and another coil (secondary coil) is connected across the load. The transformer becomes ideal when the idea of cosine approach is applied, which implies the no loss of magnetic energy. In other words, the magnetic energy remains constant when the primary and secondary coils are properly designed.
Mathematically, the relationship between power input and power output can be represented by the following formula:
Power Output (P2) = Power Input (P1) * (N2/N1)^2
where N1 and N2 are the turns ratio of primary and secondary coils, respectively.
From the above formula, we can infer that if the turns ratio is non-zero (N1!= N2), the power output will be a function of the power input and the square of the turns ratio.
So, based on the given formula, power output will always be greater than the power input absolutely, regardless of the choices A, B or C.



</reasoning>
<answer>B</answer>

Not only did he expect all persons of distinction to be in continual attendance at Court, but he was quick to notice the absence of those of inferior degree; at his lever, his couches, his meals, in the gardens of Versailles (the only place where the courtiers in general were allowed to follow him), he used to cast his eyes to right and left; nothing escaped him[;] he saw everybody. If anyone habitually living at Court absented himself he insisted on knowing the reason; those who came there only for flying visits had also to give a satisfactory explanation; anyone who seldom or never appeared there was certain to incur his displeasure. If asked to bestow a favor on such persons he would reply haughtily: "I do not know him"; of such as rarely presented themselves he would say, "He is a man I never see"; and from these judgments there was no appeal.
No one understood better than Louis XIV the art of enhancing the value of a favor by his manner of bestowing it; he knew how to make the most of a word, a smile, even of a glance.
He loved splendor, magnificence, and profusion in all things, and encouraged similar tastes in his Court; to spend money freely on equipages and buildings, on feasting and at cards, was a sure way to gain his favor, perhaps to obtain the honor of a word from him. Motives of policy had something to do with this; by making expensive habits the fashion, and, for people in a certain position, a necessity, he compelled his courtiers to live beyond their income, and gradually reduced them to depend on his bounty for the means of subsistence.

Not only did he expect all persons of distinction to be in continual attendance at Court, but he was quick to notice the absence of those of inferior degree; at his lever, his couches, his meals, in the gardens of Versailles (the only place where the courtiers in general were allowed to follow him), he used to cast his eyes to right and left; nothing escaped him[;] he saw everybody. If anyone habitually living at Court absented himself he insisted on knowing the reason; those who came there only for flying visits had also to give a satisfactory explanation; anyone who seldom or never appeared there was certain to incur his displeasure. If asked to bestow a favor on such persons he would reply haughtily: "I do not know him"; of such as rarely presented themselves he would say, "He is a man I never see"; and from these judgments there was no appeal.
No one understood better than Louis XIV the art of enhancing the value of a favor by his manner of bestowing it; he knew how to make the most of a word, a smile, even of a glance.
He loved splendor, magnificence, and profusion in all things, and encouraged similar tastes in his Court; to spend money freely on equipages and buildings, on feasting and at cards, was a sure way to gain his favor, perhaps to obtain the honor of a word from him. Motives of policy had something to do with this; by making expensive habits the fashion, and, for people in a certain position, a necessity, he compelled his courtiers to live beyond their income, and gradually reduced them to depend on his bounty for the means of subsistence.

Distillation and melting refer to related concepts, but the former is generally used for pure substance recovery, not a conversion process from solid to liquid via heat application.
